Party_Details.Name: ABB Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The three plaintiffs are part of a worldwide group of companies which bear the name ABB and which manufacture, market and sell several products for industrial and commercial uses.
The ABB Group's products include low, medium and high voltage circuit breakers.
The three plaintiffs are part of the Singapore component of the ABB Group, collectively referred to as the ABB Singapore Group.
The first plaintiff, ABB Holdings Pte Ltd, is owned by ABB Asea Brown Boveri Ltd, a Swiss company.
The first plaintiff was set up in Singapore to be the holding company of the Singapore businesses of the ABB Group.
The first plaintiff is the sole shareholder of the second and third plaintiffs.
On 19 July 1996 the first plaintiff entered into an employment agreement with the defendant covering the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000.

Clause 1 of the 19 July 1996 employment agreement provided that the defendant was subject to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation and would faithfully serve the Country Organisation as President of the second plaintiff with location in Singapore.
Clause 1 of the 19 July 1996 employment agreement further provided that the defendant would use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.
On 20 September 1999 the first plaintiff issued a second employment contract to the defendant, effective from 1 February 2000, and the letter was signed by both the first and second plaintiffs.
The 20 September 1999 employment contract stated that the employing entity was the second plaintiff and that the defendant's position was President.
The 20 September 1999 employment contract provided that the defendant would undertake to maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters and not to divulge any information to outside members.
The 20 September 1999 employment contract provided that private participation in other business activities, especially in the employer's line of business, was strictly not allowed.

At all material times the ABB Singapore Group utilised an Employee Handbook which contained policies, terms and conditions applicable to all employees of the ABB Singapore Group, including the plaintiffs.
The Employee Handbook stated that private participation in other business activities especially related to the nature of company business was strictly not allowed.

The Employee Handbook stated that every employee had the duty to protect the company assets, confidential and proprietary information and to avoid any conflict between his personal interests and those of the company.

The Employee Handbook stated that all employees had a duty to avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the interests of the company or might cause a conflict with the performance of their duties.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees should not make or hold any investment in any supplier, customer, competitor or other enterprises if such investment might affect their business decisions on behalf of the company.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees should not accept outside work from a supplier, client or competitor or do any outside work that may adversely affect their performance or judgment on the job and should not use company time, facilities or materials for outside work.
The plaintiffs commenced this action in December 2007.

The plaintiffs asserted that by reason of his employment with the second and third plaintiffs, the defendant owed all the plaintiffs certain express and implied fiduciary and other duties and that he had breached those duties, causing damage to the plaintiffs.

By consent of the parties, the trial was focused solely on issues relating to the defendant's liability, with issues regarding quantum to be determined in a separate hearing if liability was established.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant's employment contracts contained express terms requiring him to use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the plaintiffs.
In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to avoid any conflict between his personal interests and those of the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the plaintiffs' interests or might cause a conflict with the performance of his duties.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to refrain from participating in any business activities, especially those in the plaintiffs' line of business.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to refrain from making or holding any investment in any competitor or other entity if such investment might affect his business decisions on behalf of the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to maintain and preserve the secrecy of all matters and information of the plaintiffs and not divulge the same to any third party.
In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to refrain from sharing proprietary information of the plaintiffs with any third party.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required to protect the plaintiffs' assets, confidential and proprietary information, including those representing a substantial investment of resources of the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 9 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant was required not to appropriate for his own use or make available to others any property for purposes not of the plaintiffs.
In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed implied duties including a duty of good faith and fidelity to the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed an implied duty not to misuse any information confidential to the plaintiffs or to disclose such information to any third party.

In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed an implied duty not to use or disclose any trade secrets or other highly confidential information of the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed an implied duty to disclose to the plaintiffs any potential threat or competitive risk posed to their business.
In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed an implied duty to act in the best interests of the plaintiffs.
In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed an implied duty to use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 10 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed an implied duty not to make improper use of any information acquired by virtue of his position as an officer or agent of the company to gain an advantage for himself or any other person or to cause detriment to the company.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, pleaded in the alternative, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed them fiduciary duties including a duty to act in good faith and in the best interests of the plaintiffs.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed a fiduciary duty not to place himself in a position where his duty to the plaintiffs and his personal interests might conflict.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed a fiduciary duty to disclose to the plaintiffs information which came to him and which was of concern to the plaintiffs and was relevant for the plaintiffs to know.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed a fiduciary duty not to disclose to third parties information of concern to the plaintiffs and which was relevant for the plaintiffs to know.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed a fiduciary duty to disclose to the plaintiffs any potential threat and competitive risk posed to their business.

In paragraph 11 of the statement of claim, the plaintiffs alleged that the defendant owed a fiduciary duty to inform the plaintiffs of any activity, actual or threatened, which could damage the plaintiffs' interests.
The plaintiffs pleaded that on 18 February 2002 the defendant's brother, Sher Hock Sing, and two others procured the incorporation of Great Vision International Pte Ltd.
The plaintiffs pleaded that on 24 May 2002 the defendant procured the incorporation of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd.

The plaintiffs pleaded that in December 2002, while the defendant was employed by the plaintiffs, he exchanged email correspondence with Guenther Leonhardt in relation to enquiries by Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute regarding whether Mr Leonhardt would act as XIHARI's technical adviser in research and development projects relating to a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters.

The plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant failed to disclose to them his communications with XIHARI and Mr Leonhardt or his involvement in the establishment of Huadian or the potential threat and competitive risk posed to the plaintiffs' business by XIHARI, Huadian or other parties.

The plaintiffs pleaded that sometime in 2002 the defendant obtained a loan of RMB70,000 purportedly for travelling expenses from Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd and failed to inform the plaintiffs of this loan or the expenses it purportedly related to.
The plaintiffs pleaded that in May 2003 the defendant procured the incorporation of Gelpag International Pte Ltd and became a director and shareholder of that company.

The plaintiffs pleaded that in March 2003 the defendant was appointed general manager of Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd, a company incorporated on 4 December 2002 whose founding shareholders were XIHARI and Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd.
The plaintiffs pleaded that by 28 March 2004 the shareholders in Huadian were Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd with 80.5%, XIHARI with 3.3% and Gelpag mbH with 16.2%.

The plaintiffs pleaded that within a few months after its incorporation in December 2002, Huadian made significant progress in the manufacture, marketing, approval and sale of switchgears, circuit breakers and related parts or products similar to those of the plaintiffs and the ABB Group in terms of characteristics, design, specifications and layout.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, as a senior manager and director within the ABB Singapore Group, owed contractual and fiduciary duties to the plaintiffs, including duties arising from the Employee Handbook, and whether his pre‑departure conduct in relation to XIHARI, Huadian, Great Vision, Webmoney and related entities breached those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: In employment and company law, the contractual intention of the employer and employee determines whether external documents such as handbooks form part of the employment contract, and such documents may be incorporated expressly or by inference where they are apt to be contractual terms and are treated as governing employment conditions. Senior officers and de facto or shadow directors may owe fiduciary duties analogous to those owed by formally appointed directors, including duties of loyalty, good faith, avoidance of conflicts of interest, and duties to disclose to the company information and opportunities that are of concern to it. Employees owe duties of fidelity not to engage, while employed, in competing business activities or preparatory acts that materially assist a competing business, and must not use their position to advance external ventures in conflict with their employer's interests. Clauses prohibiting private participation in other business activities, especially in the employer's line of business, and requiring protection of confidential and proprietary information, are enforceable contractual obligations when properly incorporated.
Party_Details.Application: The first plaintiff, as a non‑trading holding company, was party to the initial employment contracts but did not itself conduct operational business in the relevant product markets, and the pleaded duties and alleged misconduct primarily concerned the operational activities of the second and third plaintiffs. The Employee Handbook, although applicable across ABB Singapore Group entities, functioned as the source of general employment terms rather than creating a distinct layer of fiduciary obligations owed specifically to the holding company. The alleged breaches, including the defendant's dealings with XIHARI, Huadian, Great Vision, Webmoney, and related entities, related to product development, marketing and competitive conduct in low and medium voltage markets, which were carried on by the second and third plaintiffs rather than by the first plaintiff. On these facts, the general principles on incorporation of handbook terms and on fiduciary obligations of senior managers did not extend to impose separate fiduciary duties owed to the holding company in its capacity as such, beyond the express contractual framework already identified.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The first plaintiff's role as a holding company and the absence of direct operational involvement in the relevant businesses meant that, on the facts and contractual framework, no separate fiduciary or additional contractual duties were established as owed specifically to it in respect of the impugned conduct, and its claim did not succeed.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court examined the structure of the ABB Singapore Group and noted that the first plaintiff functioned as a holding company without direct commercial operations in the relevant product lines. Although the first plaintiff was the counterparty to the initial employment contracts, the operative employer for the period of the alleged misconduct was the second and then the third plaintiff. The judge analysed the incorporation of the Employee Handbook into the employment contracts, finding that it formed part of the general terms of employment across ABB Singapore Group entities, but emphasised that the existence of group‑wide policies did not automatically create fiduciary obligations owed to every group company. In assessing fiduciary duties, the court focused on the defendant's positions and responsibilities in the operating companies, particularly his directorship in the second plaintiff and senior management role in the third plaintiff, and concluded that fiduciary duties arose in favour of those entities because they carried on the businesses potentially affected by his conduct. The judge reasoned that there was no evidential basis to extend similar fiduciary obligations to the first plaintiff, as no specific powers, responsibilities or decision‑making authority of the defendant in relation to the holding company were shown. The court therefore confined the analysis of breaches to duties owed to the second and third plaintiffs and rejected the first plaintiff's attempt to rely on group‑wide notions of interest promotion to ground a separate claim.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The second plaintiff, ABB Installation Materials (East Asia) Pte Ltd, is a wholly owned subsidiary of the first plaintiff.
The second plaintiff manufactures miniature circuit breakers, which are low voltage products.
Between January 1997 and January 2001, the defendant's employer was the second plaintiff.
From January 1997 to January 2000, the defendant held the position of President of the second plaintiff.
From July 1998 to February 2003, the defendant was a director of the second plaintiff.
The 19 July 1996 employment contract between the first plaintiff and the defendant provided that the defendant would serve as President of the second plaintiff with location in Singapore.
The 19 July 1996 employment contract subjected the defendant to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation.
The 20 September 1999 employment contract, effective 1 February 2000, stated that the employing entity was the second plaintiff and that the defendant's position was President.
The 20 September 1999 employment contract provided that the defendant would undertake to maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters and not to divulge any information to outside members.
The 20 September 1999 employment contract provided that private participation in other business activities, especially in the employer's line of business, was strictly not allowed.
The Employee Handbook used by the ABB Singapore Group stated that private participation in other business activities especially related to the nature of company business was strictly not allowed.

The Employee Handbook stated that every employee had the duty to protect the company assets, confidential and proprietary information and to avoid any conflict between his personal interests and those of the company.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees had a duty to avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the interests of the company or might cause a conflict with the performance of their duties.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees should not make or hold any investment in any supplier, customer, competitor or other enterprises if such investment might affect their business decisions on behalf of the company.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees should not accept outside work from a supplier, client or competitor or do any outside work that may adversely affect their performance or judgment on the job and should not use company time, facilities or materials for outside work.

According to evidence, by January 2001 the second plaintiff had transferred most of its business activities to other entities but remained the holding company of facilities in Batam and Jakarta which assembled miniature circuit breakers.
The manufacturing facility in Batam owned by the second plaintiff closed down in 2002.

The plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant owed fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff, including duties to act in good faith and in the best interests of the company, to avoid conflicts of interest, and to disclose information and potential competitive threats relevant to the company.

The plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant's conduct in relation to XIHARI and Huadian, including his communications with Guenther Leonhardt and his involvement with Huadian, adversely affected the interests of the second plaintiff.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, by virtue of his role as director and senior officer of the second plaintiff and the incorporation of the Employee Handbook into his employment terms, the defendant owed fiduciary and contractual duties to the second plaintiff regarding conflicts of interest, disclosure of relevant information and non‑assistance to potential competitors, and whether his pre‑departure dealings with XIHARI, Huadian and related entities contravened those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors owe fiduciary duties to their companies, including duties to act in good faith in the interests of the company, to avoid placing themselves in positions where their personal interests conflict with their duties, and to disclose to the company information and opportunities that are of concern to it and relevant for it to know. Senior managers who effectively form part of top management and exercise substantial control over a company's business may owe fiduciary obligations similar to those of directors. Contractual terms and staff handbooks can impose additional obligations, such as prohibitions on private participation in other business activities, especially in the employer's line of business, and duties to protect confidential and proprietary information and avoid conflicts of interest. These duties constrain a director or senior manager from assisting or preparing competing ventures while still in office and require disclosure of activities and plans that may pose competitive risks to the company.
Party_Details.Application: The second plaintiff employed the defendant as President and retained him as a director until February 2003, during which time he was part of the top management of the ABB Singapore Group and had authority over aspects of the second plaintiff's business. The Employee Handbook, incorporated via the reference to general terms and conditions in the initial contract and by the continuity of employment terms, imposed obligations on him to avoid conflicts of interest and private participation in other business activities. As a director, he owed fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff, even though its operations were later narrowed to low voltage assembly facilities in Indonesia. The factual matrix shows that his interactions with XIHARI and Huadian concerned medium voltage products and a new manufacturing venture, and that these activities were primarily connected to the third plaintiff's business area and to ABB's medium voltage segment rather than to the second plaintiff's residual low voltage assembly operations. While the general fiduciary principles applied to his directorship of the second plaintiff, the specific conduct at issue related only tangentially, if at all, to the second plaintiff's remaining business scope and markets, limiting the practical impact of those duties in this context.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The defendant, as a director, owed fiduciary duties to the second plaintiff and was subject to the general employment obligations derived from the Employee Handbook, but the alleged pre‑departure conduct in relation to XIHARI and Huadian was principally connected to medium voltage activities outside the second plaintiff's narrowed operational sphere, so any actionable breaches were more directly referable to duties owed to the third plaintiff.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court identified that the defendant served as a director of the second plaintiff from 1998 to 2003 and accepted that, by virtue of this office, he occupied a fiduciary position vis‑à‑vis the second plaintiff. The judge considered evidence that by 2001 the second plaintiff had transferred most of its business activities and retained only low voltage assembly operations in Batam and Jakarta, and that it did not participate in the medium voltage product business in China. In analysing the scope of fiduciary duties, the court distinguished between the existence of such duties and their practical reach, noting that the second plaintiff's business was confined to low voltage products and a limited geographical area. The judge focused the substantive breach analysis on the third plaintiff, whose business and management structure were directly implicated by the defendant's dealings with XIHARI and Huadian. While acknowledging that the defendant's status as director of the second plaintiff entailed fiduciary obligations, the court reasoned that the factual allegations and evidence of competitive risk and preparatory steps for Huadian related primarily to the third plaintiff's operations and markets. As a result, the reasoning on concrete breaches and remedies was anchored in the relationship between the defendant and the third plaintiff, with the second plaintiff's position recognised but not treated as the primary locus of the impugned conduct.

Party_Details.Name: ABB Industry Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The third plaintiff, ABB Industry Pte Ltd, is a wholly owned subsidiary of the first plaintiff.
The third plaintiff carries on business as, among other things, a manufacturer of switchgears and an installer of electrical works.
From January 2001 until he left the ABB Group, the defendant was based in Singapore and employed by the third plaintiff.
From January 2001 to February 2003, the defendant held the position of General Manager of the third plaintiff.

From September 2001 to February 2003, the defendant held the position of Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South, in the third plaintiff.

On 15 January 2001, pursuant to a new organisation structure effective 1 January 2001, the defendant's employment was transferred from the second plaintiff to the third plaintiff with retrospective effect to 1 January 2001.

The third employment contract consisted of a letter dated 15 January 2001 from the third plaintiff to the defendant accompanied by a document titled 'Summary of Terms and Conditions of Employment – Staff Category M1/M2'.
The 15 January 2001 letter stated that the defendant's designation in the third plaintiff was 'General Manager BAU LV Products and Systems' and his job grade was 'M1'.

The Summary attached to the 15 January 2001 letter contained a provision that the management maintained the right to review, change, amend, delete or add to the policies, terms and conditions of employment as the management deemed fit.

The Employee Handbook used by the ABB Singapore Group contained provisions on transfer and deployment, stating that an employee could be transferred or redeployed within or to another company, division or department to make better use of his knowledge and expertise.

The Employee Handbook stated that every employee had the duty to protect company assets, confidential and proprietary information and to avoid any conflict between personal interests and those of the company.
The Employee Handbook stated that private participation in other business activities especially related to the nature of company business was strictly not allowed.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees had a duty to avoid financial, business or other relationships which might be opposed to the interests of the company or might cause a conflict with the performance of their duties.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees should not make or hold any investment in any supplier, customer, competitor or other enterprises if such investment might affect their business decisions on behalf of the company.

The Employee Handbook stated that employees should not accept outside work from a supplier, client or competitor or do any outside work that may adversely affect their performance or judgment on the job and should not use company time, facilities or materials for outside work.

The defendant was part of the top management of the ABB Singapore Group and attended meetings of the Country Management Team where the plaintiffs' various businesses, including medium voltage products, were discussed.
The defendant had authority to hire and fire employees of the third plaintiff.
The third plaintiff was involved in the sale of medium voltage products, including supplying such products as part of projects or packages.

The defendant agreed in evidence that if, in the course of his employment with the third plaintiff, he had been contacted by a potential customer for medium voltage products, he would normally have passed the customer and information to the manager responsible for those products.

The plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant owed the third plaintiff express contractual duties, including duties to avoid conflicts of interest, to refrain from participating in other business activities, and to maintain secrecy of office matters.

The plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant owed the third plaintiff implied duties of good faith and fidelity, duties not to misuse confidential information, and duties to disclose potential threats and competitive risks to the third plaintiff's business.

The plaintiffs pleaded that the defendant owed the third plaintiff fiduciary duties, including duties to act in good faith and in the best interests of the company, to avoid conflicts of interest, and to disclose information and activities that could damage the company's interests.
On 18 February 2002, Great Vision International Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore, and the initial shareholders and directors included the defendant's brother, Sher Hock Sing.
Great Vision International Pte Ltd was incorporated to undertake a property development project in Xiamen, China through a subsidiary project company.
In 2002, Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd was established in China as a subsidiary of Great Vision to carry out the Xiamen property development project.
The defendant learned of an opportunity to develop a plot of land in Xiamen from Mayor Hong Yong Shi and passed this opportunity to Mr Lin Qunxing and his brother, Sher Hock Sing.

In 2002, Xiamen Fu Cheng's financial statements recorded an outstanding amount of RMB70,000 described as a travel expenses loan in the defendant's name, with a payment voucher dated 3 December 2002 reflecting the defendant as payee and applicant and payment received by Huang Jianhuang.
On 24 May 2002, Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore with directors including Luo Baocheng and Chen Wei Ting, and its registered address was the defendant's home address.
The defendant assisted in the incorporation of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd by filing documents with the authorities and providing his address as its registered office.

Between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003, nine emails were exchanged between the defendant and former ABB employee Guenther Leonhardt concerning XIHARI's enquiries about engaging a technical adviser for research and development projects relating to a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters.
In late October 2002, the defendant attended the 10th anniversary celebrations of ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd in Xiamen, China.

During the October 2002 event, representatives of Xian High Voltage Apparatus Research Institute, including Li Zhao Lin and Pei Zhengjiang, informed the defendant that XIHARI was looking for a technical adviser for its research and development projects.

In November 2002, XIHARI's General Manager, Pei Zhengjiang, and another officer, Zheng Jun, had telephone conversations with the defendant about engaging an internationally known technical adviser and mentioned Guenther Leonhardt as a candidate.
The defendant communicated XIHARI's interest to Guenther Leonhardt, who responded positively to the possibility of acting as technical adviser.

In or about December 2002, Yong Ying (Xiamen) Science and Technology Co Ltd and XIHARI entered into a joint design project to jointly design, develop, produce and manufacture medium voltage vacuum circuit breakers with oval moulded poles.

In November 2002, Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd and XIHARI entered into a joint venture agreement to advance the joint project by setting up a new company to handle the commercial exploitation of the new products.
On 4 December 2002, Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd was incorporated in China as a joint venture company, with initial shareholders including XIHARI and Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd.
By 31 January 2003, Huadian had finished prototyping and the most critical type testing of its vacuum circuit breaker product, with testing having begun on 25 October 2002.
The testing of Huadian's first set of vacuum circuit breakers was fully completed on 13 March 2003.
In January 2003, businessman Liu Chunguang approached the defendant to offer him the position of general manager of Huadian.
By the end of January 2003, the defendant accepted the offer to become general manager of Huadian.
The defendant's employment with the third plaintiff ended in February 2003.
On 3 March 2003, the defendant's appointment as general manager of Huadian took effect.
On 3 March 2003, the board of directors of Huadian held its first board meeting at which the defendant made a presentation of forecasts and proposals for Huadian's business.

In March 2003, the defendant invited Guenther Leonhardt to visit Huadian's premises in Xiamen, and Mr Leonhardt visited China in February and again in March 2003 in connection with Huadian and XIHARI.
In late March 2003, a technical consultancy agreement was signed between Huadian and Guenther Leonhardt, backdated to 1 March 2003.
In May 2003, the defendant procured the incorporation of Gelpag International Pte Ltd in Singapore and became a director and shareholder of that company.
In October 2003, Gelpag mbH was incorporated in Germany, with the defendant holding a majority shareholding and Guenther Leonhardt holding a minority shareholding.
In late March 2004, Gelpag mbH purchased 16.2% of the shares in Huadian at par value.
By 28 March 2004, the shareholders of Huadian were Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd with 80.5%, XIHARI with 3.3% and Gelpag mbH with 16.2%.
The defendant used part of the profits he obtained from the Xiamen property development project to fund his investment in Gelpag mbH, which in turn invested in Huadian.
Huadian is a manufacturer of switchgears and circuit breakers, in particular medium voltage circuit breakers.
Huadian commenced marketing its vacuum circuit breaker in July 2003 and received its first order for that product in September 2003.

Huadian's website stated that it was aggressive in developing global markets and that its products had been sold in Southeast Asia, Africa and Europe, with liaison offices and partner channels in several countries including Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand.
The defendant agreed in cross-examination that Huadian was a competitor of the plaintiffs in relation to medium voltage products.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the defendant, as General Manager and Vice‑President of the third plaintiff and a member of its top management, owed contractual and fiduciary duties to avoid conflicts of interest, to refrain from private participation in other business activities, and to disclose potential competitive threats, and whether his pre‑departure conduct in assisting XIHARI and Huadian and preparing to run Huadian breached those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: Senior employees who occupy top management positions and exercise substantial control over a company's business may owe fiduciary duties analogous to those of directors, including duties to act in good faith in the interests of the company, to avoid placing themselves in positions where their personal interests may conflict with their duties, and to disclose to the company information and activities that are of concern to it and relevant for it to know. An employee's contractual obligations, including those incorporated from an employee handbook, can require protection of company assets and confidential information, avoidance of conflicts of interest, and prohibition of private participation in other business activities, especially in the employer's line of business. A director or senior manager who intends to engage in a competing business must not, while still in office, take preparatory steps that amount to active assistance in establishing or advancing the competing venture, and must inform the company of activities, actual or threatened, that may damage its interests, particularly where those activities involve third parties and potential competitors.
Party_Details.Application: The defendant held the positions of General Manager and Vice‑President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products in the third plaintiff, attended Country Management Team meetings, and had authority to hire and fire staff, placing him within the top management of the company. The Employee Handbook, incorporated into his employment terms, imposed obligations to avoid conflicts of interest and private participation in other business activities, and to protect confidential and proprietary information. As a very senior employee, he owed fiduciary duties to the third plaintiff similar to those owed by directors. The factual record shows that while still employed by the third plaintiff, the defendant engaged in a series of acts connected to XIHARI and Huadian, including facilitating XIHARI's engagement of Guenther Leonhardt as technical adviser for the development of a new generation of medium voltage products, communicating with XIHARI's officers about this engagement, and preparing business plans and forecasts for Huadian's operations. These activities related to medium voltage products and markets in which the third plaintiff was involved, at least through project supply, and Huadian was positioned as a competitor in those markets. By assisting XIHARI to secure a key technical consultant and by working on Huadian's business plans while still employed, the defendant took preparatory steps that advanced a competing venture. He also did not disclose to the third plaintiff XIHARI's intention to develop and market new medium voltage circuit breakers and the associated commercial plans, despite this information being of concern to the third plaintiff's business. In contrast, his involvement in the Xiamen property development project through Great Vision and Xiamen Fu Cheng, and his nominal assistance in incorporating Webmoney, related to property and corporate structuring activities outside the third plaintiff's line of business and did not materially interfere with his duties or create direct competition, so those activities did not substantively engage the core fiduciary and contractual prohibitions.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The defendant, as a very senior manager of the third plaintiff, owed fiduciary and contractual duties to avoid conflicts of interest, to refrain from private participation in competing business activities, and to disclose potential competitive threats, and his undisclosed assistance to XIHARI in securing a technical adviser for medium voltage product development and his preparatory work on Huadian's business while still employed constituted breaches of those duties, whereas his property development and Webmoney-related activities did not materially infringe the third plaintiff's protected interests.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court first determined that the Employee Handbook formed part of the defendant's employment terms throughout his tenure with the ABB Singapore Group, including after his transfer to the third plaintiff, by analysing the contractual references to general terms and conditions, the continuity of employment, the job grade system, and the defendant's own admissions about the Handbook governing his practice. The judge then examined the defendant's roles and responsibilities within the third plaintiff, noting his positions as General Manager and Vice‑President, his participation in Country Management Team meetings, and his authority over staffing, and concluded that he was part of the company's top management. Drawing on authorities concerning senior managers and de facto directors, the court held that such a senior employee owed fiduciary duties to the third plaintiff akin to those of a director, in addition to contractual duties of fidelity and compliance with handbook policies. In assessing the scope of these duties, the judge rejected the defendant's attempt to confine them strictly to low voltage products and the Asia South region, pointing out that the third plaintiff did sell medium voltage products as part of projects and that the defendant was privy to information about that segment through management meetings. The court then scrutinised the defendant's conduct in relation to XIHARI and Huadian. It found that the email correspondence and meetings showed that the defendant knew XIHARI intended to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers for China and potentially other Asian markets, and that XIHARI sought to engage Guenther Leonhardt as technical adviser and technical controller for a manufacturing company. The judge reasoned that this plan posed a potential competitive threat to the third plaintiff's medium voltage activities and that the information was of concern to the company. By facilitating XIHARI's engagement of Mr Leonhardt and not informing the third plaintiff of these developments, the defendant failed to act in the company's best interests and breached his fiduciary and contractual duties. The court rejected the argument that XIHARI's status as a research institute or the alleged lower quality of Chinese products eliminated competitive risk, noting evidence from Huadian's own marketing materials and the defendant's statements that Huadian aimed to produce products on par with world‑class offerings and to compete internationally. The judge also inferred from the timing and content of the defendant's presentation at Huadian's first board meeting that he had spent time before leaving the third plaintiff preparing business plans and forecasts for Huadian, which amounted to taking preparatory steps to advance a competing business while still employed. In contrast, the court found insufficient evidence that the defendant actively participated in the Xiamen property development project during his employment, treating his role as limited to passing on an opportunity, and characterised any potential breach of the contractual prohibition on private participation in other business activities as technical and unlikely to have caused more than nominal loss. The Webmoney involvement was treated as de minimis, as the company remained dormant and the defendant's role was nominal. Overall, the court isolated the breaches to the defendant's failure to disclose XIHARI's plans, his assistance in securing a technical adviser for XIHARI and Huadian, and his preparatory work on Huadian's business while still employed by the third plaintiff.

Party_Details.Name: Sher Hock Guan Charles

Facts.Text: The defendant, Sher Hock Guan Charles, is a Singaporean.
The defendant worked for various companies in the ABB Group between 1990 and February 2003.
During his ABB career, the defendant worked in Xiamen, China for ABB China Ltd and ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd.
From January 1997 until he left the ABB Group, the defendant was based in Singapore.
From January 1997 to January 2000, the defendant was President of the second plaintiff.
From July 1998 to February 2003, the defendant was a director of the second plaintiff.
From January 2001 to February 2003, the defendant was General Manager of the third plaintiff.
From September 2001 to February 2003, the defendant was Vice-President and Head of Business Area Low Voltage Products, Automation Technology Products Division Asia South, in the third plaintiff.
On 19 July 1996, the defendant entered into his first employment contract with the first plaintiff, covering the period from 1 January 1997 to 31 January 2000.

Clause 1 of the 19 July 1996 contract subjected the defendant to the General Terms and Conditions of Employment of the Country Organisation and required him to serve as President of the second plaintiff and to use his best endeavours to promote the interest and welfare of the ABB Group.
On 20 September 1999, the defendant entered into a second employment contract, effective from 1 February 2000, issued by the first plaintiff and signed by both the first and second plaintiffs.
The 20 September 1999 contract stated that the defendant's position was President and that the employing entity was the second plaintiff.
The 20 September 1999 contract provided that the defendant would undertake to maintain and preserve secrecy in all office matters and not to divulge any information to outside members.
The 20 September 1999 contract provided that private participation in other business activities, especially in the employer's line of business, was strictly not allowed.

On 15 January 2001, the defendant received a letter from the third plaintiff transferring his employment from the second plaintiff to the third plaintiff with retrospective effect from 1 January 2001.
The 15 January 2001 letter stated that the defendant's designation was General Manager BAU LV Products and Systems and that his job grade was M1.

The 15 January 2001 letter enclosed a Summary of Terms and Conditions of Employment – Staff Category M1/M2, which included a clause reserving to management the right to review, change, amend, delete or add to the policies, terms and conditions of employment.
The defendant had access to and was aware of the ABB Singapore Group Employee Handbook at all material times.
The defendant agreed in evidence that the Employee Handbook should govern his practice in the ABB Singapore companies.

The defendant was responsible for the general management of the business and for business development, marketing and sales of installation materials and low voltage products in the Asia Pacific region.
The defendant attended meetings of the Country Management Team where the ABB Singapore Group's various businesses, including medium voltage products, were discussed.
The defendant had authority to hire and fire employees in the ABB Singapore Group companies for which he was responsible.
On 18 February 2002, Great Vision International Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore, with initial shareholders and directors including the defendant's brother, Sher Hock Sing, and two others.
Great Vision International Pte Ltd was incorporated to undertake a property development project in Xiamen, China.
The defendant learned from Xiamen Mayor Hong Yong Shi of an opportunity to develop a plot of land in Xiamen and passed this opportunity to his brother and to businessman Lin Qunxing.
In July 2002, Xiamen Fu Cheng Real Estate Property Development Co. Ltd was established in China as a subsidiary of Great Vision to carry out the Xiamen property development project.
The defendant's home address was used as the registered address of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd upon its incorporation on 24 May 2002.
The defendant assisted in the incorporation of Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd by filing incorporation documents and acting as a local contact.
In late October 2002, the defendant attended the 10th anniversary celebrations of ABB Xiamen Switchgear Co Ltd in Xiamen, China.

During the October 2002 event, XIHARI representatives, including Chairman Li Zhao Lin and General Manager Pei Zhengjiang, informed the defendant that XIHARI was looking for a technical adviser for its research and development projects.

In November 2002, XIHARI's General Manager, Pei Zhengjiang, and officer Zheng Jun had telephone conversations with the defendant about engaging an internationally known technical adviser and mentioned former ABB engineer Guenther Leonhardt as a candidate.

The defendant contacted Guenther Leonhardt and informed him of XIHARI's interest in engaging him as a technical adviser for research and development projects relating to a new generation of medium voltage switchgear, circuit breakers and vacuum interrupters.
Between 3 December 2002 and 3 February 2003, nine emails were exchanged between the defendant and Guenther Leonhardt concerning XIHARI's proposal and the terms of a possible engagement.
On 4 December 2002, Xiamen Huadian Switchgear Co. Ltd was incorporated in China as a joint venture company with initial shareholders including XIHARI and Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd.
By 31 January 2003, Huadian had completed prototyping and the most critical type testing of its vacuum circuit breaker product, with testing having begun on 25 October 2002.
The testing of Huadian's first set of vacuum circuit breakers was fully completed on 13 March 2003.
In January 2003, businessman Liu Chunguang approached the defendant to offer him the position of general manager of Huadian.
By the end of January 2003, the defendant accepted the offer to become general manager of Huadian.
The defendant's employment with the third plaintiff ended in February 2003.
On 3 March 2003, the defendant's appointment as general manager of Huadian took effect.
On 3 March 2003, the board of directors of Huadian held its first board meeting at which the defendant made a presentation of forecasts and proposals for Huadian's business.
In early March 2003, the defendant invited Guenther Leonhardt to visit Huadian's premises in Xiamen to discuss potential technical consultancy work.
In late March 2003, a technical consultancy agreement was signed between Huadian and Guenther Leonhardt, backdated to 1 March 2003.
In May 2003, the defendant procured the incorporation of Gelpag International Pte Ltd in Singapore and became a director and shareholder of that company.
In October 2003, Gelpag mbH was incorporated in Germany, with the defendant holding a majority shareholding and Guenther Leonhardt holding a minority shareholding.
In late March 2004, Gelpag mbH purchased 16.2% of the shares in Huadian at par value.
By 28 March 2004, the shareholders of Huadian were Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd with 80.5%, XIHARI with 3.3% and Gelpag mbH with 16.2%.
Huadian had a registered capital of RMB30 million, and Gelpag mbH's 16.2% shareholding corresponded to a capital contribution of RMB4.86 million.

The defendant owned the majority of shares in Gelpag mbH and funded his portion of the RMB4.86 million investment in Huadian using profits he had obtained from the Xiamen property development project.

In his defence, the defendant denied that his employment contracts contained the full range of express terms alleged by the plaintiffs, save for the best endeavours clause in the 19 July 1996 agreement and the secrecy and non-participation clauses in the 20 September 1999 agreement.
The defendant denied owing the implied duties and fiduciary duties pleaded by the plaintiffs in paragraphs 10 and 11 of the statement of claim.
The defendant asserted that between January 1997 and February 2003 he was involved only in the business of low voltage products and had no involvement in medium and high voltage products.
The defendant asserted that he had no access to confidential technical information or confidential information pertaining to the manufacturing process of medium or high voltage products.

The defendant asserted that Great Vision International Pte Ltd and Webmoney Investment (S) Pte Ltd were not engaged in any business in competition with the plaintiffs or which would affect their interests.
The defendant asserted that his correspondence with Guenther Leonhardt was not in breach of any duty owed to the plaintiffs.
The defendant asserted that the RMB70,000 recorded in Xiamen Fu Cheng's accounts was a cash advance for travelling expenses of his brother and Huang Jianhuang and was wrongly attributed to him.

The defendant asserted that there were no non-competition clauses or restrictive covenants in his employment agreements with the plaintiffs and that his incorporation of Gelpag International Pte Ltd and his acceptance of the position of general manager of Huadian after leaving the plaintiffs' employment were not in breach of any duty.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, given his senior roles within the ABB Singapore Group and the terms of his employment, the defendant owed contractual and fiduciary duties to the plaintiffs to avoid conflicts of interest, to refrain from private participation in competing business activities, and to disclose potential competitive threats, and whether his conduct in relation to XIHARI, Huadian, Great Vision, Webmoney and related entities prior to leaving ABB breached those duties.
Party_Details.Rule: An employee's contractual obligations are determined by the express terms of the employment agreements and any incorporated documents such as staff handbooks, which may impose duties of secrecy, prohibitions on private participation in other business activities, and requirements to avoid conflicts of interest. Directors and very senior managers who are part of a company's top management owe fiduciary duties to act in good faith in the interests of the company, to avoid placing themselves in positions where their personal interests may conflict with their duties, and to disclose to the company information and activities that are of concern to it and relevant for it to know. While employees may plan to leave and subsequently compete, they must not, while still employed, take preparatory steps that amount to active assistance in establishing or advancing a competing business, nor may they assist third parties in activities that pose a potential competitive threat to their employer without disclosure.
Party_Details.Application: The defendant's employment history and positions show that he progressed from President and director of the second plaintiff to General Manager and Vice‑President of the third plaintiff, with responsibilities over low voltage products and participation in top‑level management meetings that included discussion of medium voltage activities. The express terms of his contracts, together with the incorporated Employee Handbook, imposed obligations of secrecy, non‑participation in other business activities, and avoidance of conflicts of interest. As a director of the second plaintiff and a very senior manager of the third plaintiff, he owed fiduciary duties to those companies. The factual record indicates that while still employed by the third plaintiff, the defendant facilitated XIHARI's efforts to engage Guenther Leonhardt as a technical adviser for the development of a new generation of medium voltage products and for a manufacturing company that would market those products in China and potentially other Asian markets. He engaged in multiple communications with XIHARI's officers and with Mr Leonhardt, and he accepted an offer to become general manager of Huadian, a new joint venture company formed by XIHARI and Fu Hsiang International Pte Ltd to commercialise the products. The timing and content of his presentation at Huadian's first board meeting on 3 March 2003 support the inference that he had prepared business plans and forecasts for Huadian before his employment with the third plaintiff ended. These actions constituted preparatory steps that actively assisted a venture positioned to compete in the medium voltage segment in which the third plaintiff operated, at least through project supply, and he did not disclose XIHARI's plans or his own intended role in Huadian to his ABB employers. By contrast, his involvement in the Xiamen property development project through Great Vision and Xiamen Fu Cheng, and his nominal assistance in incorporating Webmoney, related to property and corporate structuring activities outside the ABB Singapore Group's line of business and did not materially interfere with his performance of duties or create direct competition. His assertions that he lacked access to confidential medium voltage manufacturing information and that he did not intend to compete with the plaintiffs were considered against the objective evidence of his roles and actions.
Party_Details.Conclusion: The defendant's senior positions and the incorporated employment terms gave rise to fiduciary and contractual duties to the operating plaintiffs, and his undisclosed assistance to XIHARI in securing a technical adviser for medium voltage product development and his preparatory work on Huadian's business while still employed were inconsistent with those duties, whereas his property development and Webmoney-related activities did not amount to substantive breaches of his obligations.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court began by construing the defendant's employment contracts and the Employee Handbook to determine the scope of his contractual obligations, concluding that the Handbook formed part of his terms of employment throughout his tenure with the ABB Singapore Group. It then examined his roles as President and director of the second plaintiff and as General Manager and Vice‑President of the third plaintiff, together with his participation in Country Management Team meetings and authority over staffing, to characterise him as part of the top management of the operating companies. Applying principles from authorities on fiduciary duties of directors and senior managers, the judge held that the defendant owed fiduciary duties to the second and third plaintiffs, but not to the first plaintiff or to the ABB Group as a whole. In analysing the factual allegations, the court distinguished between the defendant's involvement in the Xiamen property development project and Webmoney, which were outside the plaintiffs' line of business and did not materially affect his performance of duties, and his conduct in relation to XIHARI and Huadian. The judge found that the email correspondence and meetings with XIHARI's officers and with Guenther Leonhardt showed that the defendant knew XIHARI intended to develop and market a new generation of medium voltage circuit breakers and to establish a manufacturing company, and that he actively assisted XIHARI in securing Mr Leonhardt as technical adviser and technical controller. The court reasoned that this plan posed a potential competitive threat to the third plaintiff's medium voltage activities and that the information was of concern to the company, triggering a duty to disclose. The judge rejected the defendant's arguments that XIHARI's status as a research institute, the alleged lower quality of Chinese products, or the absence of restrictive covenants negated his obligations, pointing to Huadian's own marketing statements and the defendant's admissions that Huadian was a competitor. The court also inferred from the sophistication of the business plan and forecasts presented at Huadian's first board meeting that the defendant had spent time before leaving ABB preparing Huadian's business, which amounted to taking preparatory steps to advance a competing venture while still employed. At the same time, the judge accepted evidence that the technical development of Huadian's products was carried out by Yong Ying and XIHARI and that the defendant, who lacked technical expertise in medium voltage manufacturing, did not supply confidential technical information. The reasoning thus separated the defendant's managerial and strategic assistance to a competitor, which engaged his fiduciary and contractual duties, from any alleged misuse of technical trade secrets, which was not substantiated on the evidence.
